notes.note: Asclapo (Greek Asklapon, a name derived from the healing god Asclepius) is otherwise unknown; he was a Greek physician at Patrae (modern Patras), a Roman colony in Achaea, who had attended members of Cicero's household and won Cicero's personal confidence.

notes.anchor: of which I have had experience in the illnesses of my own people
notes.note: "My own people" (meorum) refers to Cicero's familia, the extended household of family members and slaves under his authority; Roman upper-class households routinely retained or patronised Greek physicians, medicine being a predominantly Greek profession in Rome.

notes.anchor: he has satisfied me by his knowledge no less than by his loyalty and good will
notes.note: Cicero singles out fides (loyalty/trustworthiness) alongside scientia (medical knowledge): for ancient patients, a physician's ethical reliability was as significant as his technical skill, particularly since doctors had intimate access to a household.
